text: The surgeon focuses on the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), dividing it just below the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure. A medial-lateral plane is developed in front of the aorta fascia. Dissection proceeds under the IMV from the medial side, where the pancreas is identified and separated from the colonic splenic flexure. The omentum is then released from the transverse colon.

The surgeon navigates through adhesions from the patient's previous laparoscopic gastric sleeve surgery, continuing dissection on the left paracolic side to achieve full mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon makes an initial incision at the level of the sacral promontory to identify the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The total mesorectal excision (TME) plane is opened and followed cranially, with careful dissection alongside the IMA. The artery is traced to its origin and divided using hemolog clips.

The surgeon identifies the superior rectal artery to enter the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane posteriorly, opening the posterior plane. Dissection is performed deeply both anteriorly and posteriorly, starting one centimeter above the peritoneal reflection. The lateral dissection in TME is challenging, but early anterior and posterior dissection aids in determining the lateral plane.

The surgeon continues the dissection posteriorly down to the pelvic floor, following a sequential approach that includes posterior dissection, right lateral, anterior, and then left lateral dissection.

The surgeon divides the posterior midline roughing to allow the rectum to stretch upwards. A digital rectal examination is performed to identify the tumor level. The dissection continues along the rectovaginal septum towards the pelvis, with careful identification of the vagina.

The surgeon carefully pulls the rectum out of the pelvis using a plastic tube placed around the distal rectum to provide traction, enhancing visualization of the pelvic floor and aiding in the identification of the tumor in the distal rectum. A simultaneous digital rectal examination assists in this process.

The surgeon performs the robotic transection of the mesocolon, facilitating the extraction of the specimen through the anus. The robotic platform is undocked, and a lone star retractor is placed to enhance exposure for the perineal dissection, allowing clear visualization of the tumor.

The surgeon begins a transanal transection, preserving the sphincter complex while maintaining pneumoperitoneum in the abdomen. The anus is closed with a post-transducer to ensure a free distal margin, followed by a small elliptical incision above the sphincters. The dissection continues until the abdominal team is connected.

The intra-abdominal pressure visibly decreases as the specimen is extracted transanally through an Alexis wound protector. The surgeon performs a coloanal anastomosis using interrupted stitches of Tuo Vicryl, followed by the creation of a diverting stoma.
